arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a modest flattener (long DGS2/short DGS10, i.e., short curve) on the back of balance_sheet -0.55 and curve_slope -0.55, term_premium -0.35 — all reinforcing a flattening bias via long-end anchoring and a slope series grinding through inversion. This meeting's panel has moved meaningfully against that read. curve_slope's own analyst now reports the series ticking UP off the 12-month low (0.00 from -0.03), the first uptick after months of drift, even though medium-term momentum (63d, 252d) is still negative — the call itself is now "flat" with a slight steepening lean, down from a confident "down." Term_premium has also stalled: 10Y sits pinned at cycle lows (1.50%) with deceleration clearly evident, momentum "exhausted," and importantly the balance-sheet conclusion being pulled forward two months is flagged as a supply-relief signal that would cap upward long-end pressure — a factor that cuts against further flattening pressure from the long end. Balance_sheet itself, while still "down," is now lower conviction (0.45 vs 0.55) precisely because QT is confirmed ending within the horizon, which reduces the technical bid for the long end that had been the primary flattening engine. So all three drivers I leaned on previously have decayed toward neutral-to-slightly-steepening. None have reversed into open steepening, but the flattening thesis has clearly lost its footing: the front end has less easing-driven room left to fall relative to a long end whose supply overhang is easing. I am cutting my flattener conviction significantly and moving toward flat/near-neutral, keeping a token steepening tilt to respect the direction of change without over-committing given how noisy curve_slope's own single-point uptick is.

discounted.why: Front-end driver but flat/noisy print, no clear slope implication this month.

arbitrated.falsifier: A slope_2s10s print back below -0.05 with negative 21d change would revive the flattener; a sustained push above 0.10 or term_premium breaking above 1.70-1.80 would confirm steepening and justify adding to it.

risks.text: Single-point uptick in slope_2s10s could be noise; medium-term momentum (63d/252d) still points toward flattening.
QT-end timing details are unclear; if runoff stops sooner than assumed, long-end supply relief could steepen curve faster than priced.
Cutting a carried flattener into a single ambiguous print risks whipsawing if next month's data reasserts the down trend.

trade.rationale: Panel evidence for flattening has decayed sharply across all three drivers I previously leaned on; cutting the flattener to a token size rather than fully reversing given noisy single-point curve_slope signal.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints show a sharp reversal: total assets jumped from ~3,769,673 to 3,844,695 then 3,857,715, a +97.8bn move over 4 weeks and the 13w change is now positive (+30,898) despite prior runoff. This breaks the steady QT decline seen in the earlier observations (peak-to-trough decline of ~335bn over 52 weeks) and my prior "down" call was wrong for this most recent window — the balance sheet rose, not fell. The policy language confirms QT is being wound down: the Committee will conclude runoff in [date] two months earlier than previously signaled, which is consistent with a deliberate slowing/halting of asset reduction. The magnitude of the last two prints looks too large to be pure organic runoff-cessation drift; it likely reflects technical/settlement effects (bill purchases, repo operations, or balance-sheet composition shifts) rather than a resumption of large-scale QE, but the net direction is unambiguously up over the past month. Given the announced end of runoff is near-term and the last two data points already show rising assets, the most reasonable one-month-ahead call is that assets stay flat-to-up rather than resume decline.
Would change its mind if: A renewed decline in fed_assets next print, or explicit statement resuming/extending runoff pace, would flip this back to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

Last meeting I ran a steepener (long DGS10 / short DGS2, conviction 0.55) on the back of curve_slope +0.65 and term_premium -0.55. This meeting the evidence has shifted materially and I need to respect that. curve_slope itself has downgraded from "up" to "flat, 0.45" — the analyst explicitly notes three consecutive prints pinned at 0.47pp after the 0.68 peak, calling the prior steepening trend stalled/consolidating, not continuing. That is a direct downgrade of the driver my trade was built on. Term_premium also downgraded from "down 0.55" to "flat 0.45": the 10y proxy fell hard but has now planted at 0.70 for two straight prints, with the analyst citing a "near-term floor" given explicit QE purchase support. So the long-end anchor that was driving compression is now described as stabilizing rather than still falling.

Meanwhile balance_sheet flipped from -0.05 (flat/shrinking) to a decisive +0.85 "up" — explicit $500B+$200B forward purchase commitment, assets at 52-week high, still accelerating. This is a long-end-specific suppressive force (Treasury/MBS purchases target duration directly) and should, if anything, add mild flattening or at least anti-steepening pressure by capping the long end even as front end stays anchored near zero at the ZLB. Labor_tightness ticked up slightly (0.45 vs -0.10 previously) — signals a loosening labor market shift, which is a dovish/front-end signal (curve steepening via lower front end) but the panel's own curve_slope analyst already incorporates ZLB anchoring and calls it flat, so I don't override on this secondary read.

Given both direct slope-relevant analysts (curve_slope and term_premium) independently downgraded to "flat," and the balance-sheet acceleration is a fresh flattening-leaning force on the long end, the case for holding a full steepener at prior conviction is undercut. I am not reversing to a flattener — there's no evidence of active flattening momentum, just stalled steepening — but I am trimming conviction and sizing meaningfully, keeping a much smaller steepener tilt to respect the un-refuted longer-window steepening (63d/252d still positive per curve_slope) while acknowledging the immediate stall.

For the next monthly report, the key question is whether this level persists, extends further, or partially reverses. Historically, shock-driven unemployment spikes of this magnitude tend to keep rising for at least one more reading before stabilizing or reversing, as labor market disruptions typically take multiple months to fully register and any recovery is gradual. Given the sheer velocity and magnitude of this move, and no evidence yet of stabilization, I lean toward further deterioration or at best a plateau at this elevated level. The extreme jump makes further increase somewhat more likely than an immediate reversal, though the magnitude of continued increase is highly uncertain.
Would change its mind if: A next print back toward 8-10% or lower would signal rapid labor market recovery; a print above 15-16% would confirm continued severe deterioration.

Report:

The trend remains a clear, rapid improvement: -3.20pp over 3 months, though still +4.30pp above the 12-month low and +3.80pp above the natural rate of ~4%. The pace of improvement has been very fast (3.5→14.8→7.8), consistent with a mechanical snapback in job losses being reversed as activity resumes, and policy language now explicitly notes activity and employment "have picked up somewhat in recent months." There is no evidence of stalling or reversal in the data — each of the last five prints has declined monotonically (14.8, 13.2, 11.0, 10.2, 8.4, 7.8). Given the strength and consistency of this recovery trajectory, and no sign of deceleration in the pace of decline, I expect the unemployment rate to continue falling at the next report, though the magnitude of monthly improvement may moderate as the rate approaches more normal levels. I maintain a moderate-to-high conviction the rate will be lower next month, tempered by the still-large distance from the natural rate and by the inherent uncertainty in how quickly a snapback recovery decelerates.
Would change its mind if: A print flat or above 7.8% would signal the recovery has stalled; any rise back toward or above 10% would indicate renewed deterioration.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I carried a steepener (long DGS10/short DGS2, conviction 0.50) built on curve_slope +0.70, term_premium +0.45, balance_sheet +0.55, and labor_tightness -0.55. This meeting's panel undercuts that call materially. The curve_slope analyst itself flipped from "up 0.60" to "flat 0.35" — two consecutive unchanged prints at 0.68, short-term momentum (21d/63d) barely positive and decelerating, with the spread near the top of its range suggesting exhaustion rather than continuation. That's the direct evidence my own falsifier condition (loss of positive momentum) is starting to bite, even though the hard 0.65 breakdown threshold hasn't triggered. Term_premium also downgraded from "up" to "flat" — the 10y move stalled at 0.84, 21d/63d changes modest, still near 12m lows. This is a long-end driver reporting no fresh upward pressure, which directly removes one leg of the steepening case. Balance_sheet stayed up-and-firm (0.60, corroborated), which is consistent with continued long-end supply absorption capping term premium — a flattening-consistent force via the QE channel, even though its own headline (assets) rises. Labor_tightness continues to tighten (unemployment still falling), which on the front end argues for a firmer 2y and hence flattening pressure, reinforcing rather than opposing the deceleration in slope. Financial conditions have eased sharply, which typically supports risk assets and could keep front-end anchored while long-end premium stays compressed — another flattening-leaning cross-read, though it's outside my direct mandate. Given both the slope driver and the term-premium driver — the two most direct long-end/slope inputs — have both downgraded to flat, I should reduce conviction and shrink the steepener materially rather than hold it at prior size. I don't yet see enough to flip to a flattener outright, since momentum readings are still nominally positive, but the edge has clearly eroded.

discounted.why: Front-end driver only partially informative on slope; used as reinforcing signal, not primary.

arbitrated.falsifier: A clear re-break of slope above 0.72 with positive 21d/63d re-acceleration, or term_premium re-confirming a break above 0.90, would restore the steepener case.

risks.text: Slope consolidation could resolve either way; two flat prints aren't yet a trend reversal.
A shift in Fed tapering language could reignite term-premium steepening quickly.
Reducing a carried steepener into consolidation risks whipsaw if slope breaks upward again.
Cross-reads from financial_conditions/inflation are outside mandate and could mislead the slope call.

trade.rationale: Shrink prior steepener sharply: both curve_slope and term_premium downgraded to flat, eroding the case, but momentum hasn't reversed enough to flip to flattener.

Level-wise, core is still 0.30pp below target, but the trajectory and short-term momentum both point toward continued acceleration rather than stabilization. Given the consistency of three consecutive strong monthly prints and confirmation from both core PCE and headline CPI, I maintain and strengthen the acceleration call.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print near zero or negative, or a drop in core_pce_yoy back toward the 0.97-1.21% range, would signal reacceleration has stalled.

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print continued the downtrend: unemployment fell to 6.20% from 6.40%, extending a steady post-pandemic-peak decline (14.80% high down to 6.20% now). My prior call for a further decline was correct — the rate did not stall or rise above 6.7%; it kept improving. The 3m and 6m changes remain solidly negative (-0.50pp and -2.20pp respectively), indicating continued labor market tightening (improvement). However, the pace of decline has slowed considerably compared to earlier months in this series (drops of 1.5-2pp per month early on vs. 0.1-0.3pp in the most recent three prints), consistent with the policy language shift noting the "pace of the recovery... has moderated in recent months." The level is still 2.2pp above the ~4% natural rate, so plenty of slack remains, but the deceleration in the pace of improvement suggests diminishing month-over-month gains going forward rather than a reversal. Sahm-rule spirit doesn't apply here since we're falling, not rising rapidly off a low. Given the deceleration but still-negative momentum, I lean toward continued modest improvement (down) but with reduced conviction versus a sharper decline scenario.
Would change its mind if: A print flat or rising above 6.2% would signal the improvement has stalled; two consecutive flat/higher months would undercut this call.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

On balance, I lean toward continued modest upward pressure into month-end, but with reduced conviction given the intra-window reversal and elevated range position suggesting the easy upside may be behind us.

This panel largely reaffirms that structure but with clearly decaying momentum on both sides of the slope trade, which argues for holding the position rather than adding to it.

curve_slope itself downgraded from steepening to "flat" (0.35 conviction): the spread pulled back from a 1.37 peak to 1.30, the first down-tick after eight straight up/flat prints, though still nowhere near the 0.90-0.92 flattening threshold I set as a falsifier. Labor_tightness flipped hard to "down" (own-measurement) meaning unemployment is falling — tighter labor market — which is a front-end hawkish/tightening-adjacent signal that would, if anything, pressure the front end up faster than the long end, arguing for flattening pressure, not steepening. Inflation running hot (core PCE 3.13%) also supports front-end pressure via anticipated Fed response, reinforcing the flattening lean rather than the steepening case I held last time.

Netting this out: the long end still has some modest upward term-premium support, but it's weaker and choppier than last meeting, while front-end pressures (tightening labor market, hot inflation prints, hawkish policy language shift) have gotten stronger. My own primary driver (curve_slope) has downgraded from "up" to "flat/slightly down." This is a genuine, name-able change from this meeting's panel — not just a single month's price wiggle, since three inputs (curve_slope's own trend, term_premium's conviction, labor_tightness's flip) all point the same direction.   You called: curve_slope -0.15, labor_tightness -0.35, term_premium +0.15

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The measurement confirms the prior call: total assets rose again, now at a new 52-week high (8,101,945), up ~198bn over 4 weeks, ~382bn over 13 weeks, and ~4.95% over the quarter. This is a clean, uninterrupted expansion trend with no deceleration visible in the last few prints (each of the last several observations is higher than the prior). The policy language is unchanged and explicitly commits to continued purchases of at least $80bn/month Treasuries and $40bn/month MBS "until substantial further progress" — i.e., still active QE with no taper signaled. The pace of the 4-week change (~198bn) is roughly consistent with, if not slightly above, the stated $120bn/month combined pace, supporting continued mechanical expansion. There is no evidence of runoff, caps, or reinvestment-only language. Given the level is already at a fresh high and the statement gives no hint of tapering, the base case for month-end is a continuation of expansion at a similar pace. This was the correct call last time and the evidence still supports it undiminished.
Would change its mind if: A statement shift to tapering/reducing the monthly purchase pace, or a sustained multi-release decline in fed_assets, would overturn this view.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at 0.30 conviction was wrong-footed: since then the spread has continued to grind lower, from 1.29 down to 1.20, with 21d and 63d changes both clearly negative (-0.26 and -0.32 pp respectively) while the 252d change (+0.72) shows this is a retracement from a much steeper regime earlier in the year. The last three prints (1.26, 1.24, 1.22, 1.20) show a fairly steady downward drift rather than noise around a level, which is a flattening signal. The spread sits at 0.67 of its 12-month range — still comfortably above the 12-month low (0.41 above it) — so this isn't inversion or even close, but the trend over the past one to three months is unambiguously toward compression. The 10s30s slope at 0.61 pp remains a positively-sloped long end, giving no signal of imminent front-driven inversion pressure bleeding into 2s10s. Given the persistent short-and-medium-term flattening momentum against a still-early stage in the range, I lean toward continued modest flattening into month-end rather than a stabilization, reversing my prior flat call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in slope_2s10s back above ~1.26-1.28 sustained over 2+ readings, or a flip in slope_change_21d to positive, would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially flattened at -0.69 for the last five readings after a gradual decline from -0.65. The 4-week change is exactly 0.00, indicating momentum has stalled, and the 13-week change of -0.05 reflects easing that has already fully played out and stopped rather than an ongoing trend. The index sits near its 52-week low (nfci_above_52w_low = 0.00) and range_position of 0.01, meaning conditions are as loose as they've been in a year, but there's no further easing momentum visible — the series has bottomed out at this level for six consecutive prints. Policy language remains unchanged, reaffirming accommodative conditions but not signaling a new easing impulse. My prior call of "down" was right through the flattening period but the driver has now clearly stopped moving. With zero recent momentum and the index pinned at a floor, the most defensible call is flat: conditions are historically loose but not currently loosening further, and there's no evidence of imminent tightening either. This is a case where the easing trend has exhausted itself at a plateau, and continuation of that plateau is the best single-month expectation absent new catalysts.
Would change its mind if: A print moving off -0.69 by more than 0.02 in either direction, or nfci_change_4w turning meaningfully nonzero, would signal renewed momentum.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci_13w_avg

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Breakeven 10y has stabilized in a narrow 2.30-2.34 band over the last several prints (2.30, 2.34, 2.33, 2.32, 2.32), after an initial 21d decline that has largely leveled off (be_change_63d only -0.03pp vs -0.14pp at 21d, meaning the drop occurred early and has since flattened). My prior "flat" call was directionally correct: the series did not break down further nor push higher — it settled. Current level sits +0.32pp above the 2% target and in the 82nd percentile of its 12-month range, still elevated versus the 12m low (+0.98pp above it) but well off the 12m high. The policy language tweak (past tense "having run" vs "running") is a subtle signal of the Committee viewing the below-target period as more resolved, but this is a small wording shift and doesn't clearly reflect through to breakeven pricing yet. With three consecutive readings clustered tightly around 2.32-2.33, momentum has essentially stalled — neither the down-break through 2.35ish nor a fresh push above 2.45 has materialized. I see no strong catalyst for a directional move in either direction over the next month; the market's inflation expectations look anchored modestly above target but not trending.
Would change its mind if: A break below 2.30 with renewed negative 21d momentum, or a push above 2.40, would signal a genuine directional shift.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.72
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call at 0.72 conviction was correct: headline CPI yoy jumped from 4.13 to 5.30 (+1.17pp), and the falsifier conditions (m/m cooling toward 0.2-0.3%, core PCE flattening) did not materialize — instead outgoing m/m rose to 0.85%, the highest in the series, and core PCE yoy accelerated further from 3.13 to 3.54. Momentum is still building, not peaking. The 3m annualized rates (headline 8.88%, core 6.14%) both sit far above the 12-month trend and above the latest yoy prints themselves, meaning the yoy figure has further to climb even if m/m prints merely hold flat. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing of +0.20pp confirms the most recent month accelerated versus its own trailing average rather than decelerating. Policy language shift from "below 2 percent" to "risen, largely reflecting transitory factors" corroborates that inflation has clearly broken above target, though the "transitory" framing suggests officials expect this to fade — that is a forecast I cannot yet confirm from the data, which still shows acceleration in both headline and core. Given the 0.85% outgoing print and rising 3m annualized pace, I expect the next headline yoy print to rise again.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print falling below ~0.3% or core PCE yoy flattening/reversing would indicate the acceleration has finally peaked.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, outgoing_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (down/tightening) was wrong: unemployment ticked up from 5.80% to 5.90%, a small but genuine reversal after a long steady decline. The broader trend over 12 months remains a massive improvement (-5.10pp), and the 3m/6m changes are still net negative (-0.20pp, -0.80pp), so this is not a Sahm-rule-style rapid deterioration — sahm_gap is only +0.13pp, well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recession-onset loosening. However, the uptick from the 12m low (unrate_above_12m_low +0.10pp) combined with the fact that the prior two prints were flat-then-down (6.10, 6.10, 5.80) before this reversal suggests the rapid tightening phase is stalling as the rate approaches its natural-rate proximity (distance_from_natural still 1.90pp, so there's room to keep improving, but the pace has clearly slowed). Policy language turned more optimistic ("indicators of economic activity and employment have strengthened"), which is consistent with a labor market still recovering, not deteriorating — the language doesn't support further loosening. Given one uptick after months of decline, I read this as a stall/pause rather than a trend reversal, but I no longer have confidence in continued rapid improvement. I lean flat-to-slightly-up for the next print, with low-moderate conviction given the single data point and the strong disinflationary labor trend still intact.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 5.80% would confirm continued tightening; a print above 6.1% would confirm a genuine loosening trend reversal.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call (conviction 0.25) was wrong: the 10y has drifted lower over the last month, from ~1.50 down to 1.45, with both the 21d (-0.17pp) and 63d (-0.24pp) changes negative. That said, the yield remains well within the upper half of its 12-month range (range_position 0.76), still 0.93pp above the 12m low, and 252d change is sharply positive (+0.81pp), so the longer-run trend of rising long-end premium from the Fed's ongoing $80bn/month Treasury purchases has not reversed structurally — it has just paused/consolidated. The 30s10s slope at 0.61pp is not compressing toward zero, which argues against a durable reversal per my own falsifier criteria; my stated trigger (break below 1.56 with range_position under 0.7) actually would not even register here since we're already below that level with range_position still at 0.76 — my falsifier threshold was poorly calibrated. Given the recent softening momentum but absence of a genuine structural break, I read this as a modest pause/mild downward drift rather than a resumed rise. I'll shift my call toward flat-to-down with low conviction, acknowledging the near-term data now contradicts my prior up bias.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 1.55 with renewed positive 21d/63d momentum would signal resumed upward term premium pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I was carrying a mild flattener (DGS10 +0.05/DGS2 -0.05, conviction 0.20) built on curve_slope -0.15 (flat lean), labor_tightness -0.35 (further tightening), and term_premium +0.15 (mild long-end rise). This meeting's panel confirms the flattener and strengthens it materially. curve_slope itself now reports a much firmer down call (0.45 vs prior 0.30) with three consecutive prints (1.24, 1.22, 1.20) showing steady compression, and explicitly flags its own prior call as wrong-footed — this is a direct, on-driver update in the flattening direction, the most decision-relevant input for a slope seat. term_premium flipped from up to down (10y drifted 1.50→1.45, both 21d/63d negative) — a soft long end, consistent with flattening pressure if front end doesn't fall as much. labor_tightness also flipped, from tightening (down, -0.35) to flat/stalling (+... reported as flat with slight uptick in unemployment) — a loosening labor market pulse historically supports front-end anchoring (Fed on hold) which, combined with a soft long end, nets to flattening rather than steepening. Balance_sheet remains firmly "up" (continued QE expansion), which pressures long-end term premium down (more Treasury buying = lower long yields), reinforcing the flattening thesis via suppressed long-end yields. Inflation running hot (headline 5.30% yoy, accelerating) argues for eventual front-end tightening expectations, another flattening force via the front end anchoring lower now but repricing higher rate expectations later — though this is more a Duration/front-end story. Given curve_slope's own reversal plus corroborating term_premium softness and QE-driven long-end suppression, I am increasing conviction in the flattener versus last meeting, modestly resizing the position larger while keeping the same directional structure.

discounted.why: Single uptick print, low conviction (0.30), not a clean front-vs-long slope signal on its own.

arbitrated.falsifier: A slope print back above 1.26-1.28 sustained over 2+ readings, or term_premium's 21d/63d turning clearly positive again, would flip this back toward steepening.

10s30s slope not compressing suggests flattening may be concentrated in 2s10s belly dynamics rather than a broad-based long-end phenomenon.
Curve_slope analyst admits its own prior call was wrong-footed once already this cycle, raising risk of another reversal.

trade.rationale: Modestly resized flattener (long DGS10 richening/short duration on 2s relative) reflecting reinforced flattening signal from curve_slope's own reversal plus soft term_premium and continued QE suppression of long end.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a flattener (long DGS10, short DGS2, small conviction) on the view that labor tightening plus QE-driven front-end anchoring would compress 2s10s while curve_slope's own -0.25 call reinforced flattening. This meeting's evidence undercuts that. The curve_slope analyst itself flipped: its prior "down" call was wrong, and the spread has held 1.09-1.10 for four straight prints with a positive 21d change and positive 252d trend — direct evidence of stabilization/mild steepening, not renewed flattening. Term_premium also flipped from down to up (10y drifting higher, 21d +0.10pp), consistent with modest long-end lift. Labor_tightness remains the strongest, most persistent driver (unrate at 12m low, still falling), which is a front-end anchor: a fully-employed labor market keeps the Fed's overnight rate path pinned near zero, capping DGS2 while the long end is free to drift with term premium and growth optimism. Balance sheet remains expansionary (up 0.55) which, on the rate axis, suppresses long-end yields somewhat via portfolio-balance effects — but the taper-language hint (present-tense to retrospective framing) is a genuine new signal that QE's suppressive grip may be loosening, which would let the long end lead higher — a steepening impulse. Given curve_slope's own read now says steepening, term_premium agrees, and labor_tightness's front-end anchor supports it, I judge the flattening thesis from last meeting has been meaningfully undercut. I'm flipping the position to a modest steepener, sized cautiously since the move is only just confirmed (four prints in a tight band, not yet a decisive break above 1.15).

That analyst attributes the compression to hawkish taper language pulling the front end up faster than the long end — a genuine front-vs-long divergence, not just a level move, so it's directly on-seat evidence for me.

The Fed's continued rate hikes (fed funds raised to 3-3.25%, with more increases anticipated) keep front-end yields (dgs2 +4.22%) pinned up relative to the long end (dgs10 +3.83%), which is consistent with continued curve inversion pressure rather than steepening. Absent a clear break higher, I expect the spread to stay range-bound near current inverted levels into month-end.

Headline y/y ticked up 8.19 vs 8.22 prior — essentially flat, not the decline I expected, and the mom print (0.42) came in above the 3m average, undercutting the disinflation thesis. Core PCE y/y actually rose to 5.36 from 5.11, back near its cycle high and above my own flip threshold (5.3%). The 3-month annualized core pace (5.65%) is running hotter than the y/y rate itself, signaling reacceleration in underlying momentum, not just base effects. Headline's 3m annualized (1.90%) looks tame, but that gap between core and headline (-2.83pp) reflects energy/food swings that are not persistent — core is the better signal here given the divergence, and core is clearly moving the wrong way. Headline y/y is still elevated in the upper part of its 12m range (0.71 position) and the deceleration from the 8.98 peak has stalled over the last three prints (8.46, 8.22, 8.19) rather than continuing to fall. Given core reacceleration and stalling headline disinflation, I now lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call for next month's headline print rather than continued decline.
Would change its mind if: A clear drop in headline MoM back below 0.2% alongside core PCE y/y falling back under 5.2% would restore the disinflation case.

arbitrated.notes:
The core thesis from last meeting — flattening pressure from continued QT plus rising term premium — is only half-confirmed. Balance sheet: the runoff call is reconfirmed and even more clearly stated (conviction 0.75, steady ~$18-19B/week pace, no signs of tapering). Curve_slope itself now reports "down" (flattening) at 0.35, upgraded from the prior "flat" call, directly citing the inverted spread persisting near cycle lows with negative multi-horizon momentum. That's a direct, fresh confirmation of the flattening view from the analyst who owns the slope series itself.

The complication is term_premium: last meeting I read this as "up" at 0.45 conviction, a key plank in my flattening logic (rising long-end term premium alongside front-end anchored by hikes was actually working in the same steepening-resistant direction is nuanced, but the real point was front-end pinned high by hikes and long-end also under some upward pressure). This meeting term_premium has downgraded materially to "flat" (0.25), citing 10y yields drifting down over 21d/63d despite a still-elevated 252d uptrend. That's a genuine softening of the long-end-pressure thesis. However, financial_conditions data (which curve_slope was never shown but is highly relevant) confirms continued easing, which does not obviously fight flattening — easing conditions with continued hikes and QT is consistent with curve compression.

Netting this out: front end remains anchored by continued hiking cycle and robust labor market (flat, tight), while long end shows decelerating upward momentum in term premium but the direct curve_slope reading itself has hardened toward flattening based on its own series. I keep the flattening call, modestly increasing conviction given curve_slope's own upgrade, but I do not go further since term_premium's softening removes some of the confirming long-end lift. I maintain the existing position rather than resizing it, since the panel evidence is same-direction, not reversed.

arbitrated.notes: My prior flattening thesis rested on three legs: balance-sheet runoff pushing term premium up, curve_slope momentum flattening, and term_premium rising. This meeting reshuffles two of those legs materially. Curve_slope itself now confirms deeper inversion (-0.58, monotonic re-flattening over the last three prints, sharp 252d and 63d deterioration) — this is the direct slope read and it says flatten, reinforcing rather than contradicting my prior stance. But the other two inputs that fed my flattening logic have flipped: balance_sheet reversed hard from runoff to a sharp ~366B expansion, breaking above its 13w average — that's a dovish/liquidity-supportive shift that, if sustained, argues against the "QT drives term premium up, steepens long end" logic I used before. And term_premium itself now reports down (10y pulled back to 3.48, negative 21d/63d momentum), directly undercutting the "long end sells off" steepening-pressure story. So the long end is softening in outright yield terms even as balance-sheet dynamics turn more accommodative — both point toward front-end yields staying anchored high (labor still tight, financial conditions tightening, curve_slope confirms) while the long end grinds lower or sideways. That combination is still consistent with flattening/inversion deepening, but for a different reason than last month: it's front-end-anchored-high driven now, not long-end-selloff driven. The direct curve_slope reading is the most authoritative signal here and it unambiguously says more inversion. I'll keep the flattener but trim conviction slightly given the term_premium and balance_sheet reversals muddy the long-end narrative even though the net slope signal still confirms.

The panel has moved materially against my last read, which had curve_slope at 0.00 (no view) and net position leaning flattener (long 2y/short 10y equivalent). This meeting, curve_slope itself reports a clear, multi-window-confirmed steepening (-0.73→-0.44, 21d +0.34, 63d +0.62), directly reversing its own prior "flat" call. Term_premium also flipped from flat to up (10y at 12-month high, 4.59, with positive 21d/63d momentum and a positive 30s10s slope confirming genuine long-end pressure rather than pure curve-shape noise) — this is a long-end-specific driver pushing yields up disproportionately at the back end, which is a steepening force. Balance_sheet remains firmly "down" (runoff continuing, even slightly accelerating), which is a duration/term-premium tailwind supporting further long-end pressure — again steepening, not flattening. Labor_tightness is flat (unemployment plateaued at 3.70%, deceleration in pace of loosening) — this is a front-end input but with no directional call, it doesn't argue for flattening either.

The key reconciliation: last month I had labor_tightness "up" (tightening reversal) as my flattening driver, with curve_slope neutral. Now labor_tightness has stalled to flat, removing that flattening thesis, while curve_slope and term_premium have both turned decisively toward steepening with corroborating multi-window evidence. This is not a one-month price wiggle — it's three independent drivers (curve_slope's own measurement, term_premium's long-end momentum, and balance_sheet's persistent runoff) all now aligned toward long end cheapening faster than front end. I don't see strong evidence for a rebound next month — no base effects or acceleration signals here — so I expect headline yoy to be flat-to-slightly-down, not up.

The Sahm-style gap (0.37pp) is approaching levels historically associated with turning points. This is not a single noisy tick — the last three readings (3.70, 3.70, 3.90) show consecutive increases after a period of flat oscillation between 3.40-3.60%, suggesting a genuine trend shift rather than measurement noise. While the level itself (3.90%) is still only slightly above a 4% natural rate estimate, the rate-of-change is the more important signal here per the Sahm-rule spirit, and it argues for continued loosening rather than stabilization. Policy language also shifted from "robust" to "slowed" job gains, consistent with cooling. I'm revising toward a directional call: the unemployment rate is more likely to tick up or hold at this elevated level than to reverse sharply back down next month, given the momentum evident in the trailing three prints.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 3.60-3.70% would confirm the 3.90 print was noise; another rise to 4.0%+ would confirm accelerating loosening.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2, conviction 0.55) built on three legs: continued QT (long-end supply pressure), rising term premium (long end), and a curve_slope analyst calling steepening at 0.60 conviction, with labor barely a factor (0.05). This meeting's panel largely confirms the structural pieces but softens the momentum piece that matters most for slope: curve_slope itself has downgraded from "up" 0.60 to "flat" 0.25, explicitly noting the steepening move has stalled/mildly reversed near 12-month highs (range_position 0.94) with 21d slope_change decelerating versus 63d pace. That's a direct, first-party signal that the front-vs-long balance is no longer widening at the prior pace, even though it hasn't reversed. Term_premium stays "up" but conviction fell from 0.55 to 0.40, also flagging a pause in momentum despite the multi-month rise being intact — still a long-end-supportive force but not accelerating. Balance_sheet remains a clean, high-conviction "down" (0.80), unchanged and confirming ongoing QT — a genuine long-end steepening pressure via term premium/duration supply, and the most reliable, least-stale input here. Labor_tightness flipped to "up" 0.55 (unemployment jumped to 3.90%, above my own flip-trigger of 3.80%), which on the front end argues for anticipated cuts and front-end richening — reinforcing steepening via the front end, not against it. So: three of four drivers (balance_sheet, term_premium, labor) still point toward steepening pressure, just with decelerating momentum; only curve_slope's own composite reading has cooled to flat. Given the trend is intact but decelerating, and my own falsifier (2s10s below -0.55 sustained, or unemployment 3.80%+) is now half-triggered (unemployment did cross 3.80, which argues for MORE steepening via front-end cuts, not less), I keep the steepener but trim conviction to reflect the stalling curve_slope reading.

Distance from the 2% target remains modest at +0.23pp, well within normal anchoring. Absent a new catalyst, I expect breakevens to hold near current levels rather than resume falling or bounce back sharply.
Would change its mind if: A break below 2.18 or a sustained move back above 2.28 over several prints would signal renewed directional momentum rather than plateau.

Still, with mom prints modest and no signs of reacceleration in either headline or core, I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-lower readings rather than a reversal upward. Conviction is moderate given the yoy is near cycle lows and base effects are less favorable going forward, tempering the disinflation call.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or yoy rising back above 3.4% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued disinflation.

arbitrated.notes:
Last meeting I ran a steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2, conviction 0.45) on the back of a curve_slope reading that was actually "flat" (mislabeled), balance-sheet runoff pressuring the long end, and a firming labor market. This meeting's panel largely confirms and sharpens that read, but the internal mix has shifted in an important way.

curve_slope itself now reports "up" (steepening) with 0.45 conviction, admitting its own prior flat call was wrong and that the multi-month trend (63d +0.42, 252d +0.37) has been steepening, with only a wobbly 21d pullback. That's a direct, front-vs-long read and it corroborates the steepener thesis on its own terms — this is the most load-bearing input since it's the only driver that speaks to slope directly.

term_premium reversed hard from "up" to "flat," with 10y yields pulling back sharply on 21d momentum even though the 63d/252d uptrend is intact. This is a level move that plausibly caps the long end near-term and is a genuine reason to trim conviction versus last meeting, since term_premium is a long-end driver and its deceleration works against further steepening even though it doesn't reverse it.

balance_sheet remains unambiguously "down" (runoff continuing at 0.80 conviction, unchanged policy language) — that keeps structural upward pressure on term premium/long-end supply, supporting the steepener framework even as near-term momentum has stalled.

labor_tightness downgraded from up 0.55 to essentially flat (0.30, "borderline flat/up-lean") — the 3.90 spike proved to be noise, unemployment settled back to 3.70. This softens the front-end-anchoring argument (a genuinely loosening labor market would let the Fed ease, pulling the front end down and steepening the curve) but doesn't reverse it — there's still a mild loosening drift over 12 months.

Net: the core steepening drivers (curve_slope direct read, ongoing QT) are intact or strengthened, but the term-premium momentum stall and labor cooling to flat both argue for trimming size rather than reversing. I'll hold the steepener but reduce conviction modestly, reflecting that the long-end momentum that was supposed to power this trade has paused even as the structural/supply argument persists.

=== balance_sheet ===

My prior call (down, 0.80) was correct: the latest print continued the downtrend with no flat or rising week. Policy language remains unchanged, explicitly stating the Committee will continue reducing Treasury and agency holdings per previously announced plans — no signal of pause, taper, or end of QT. Assets remain over $1T below the 52-week high and show no sign of approaching a floor. Given the consistency of the weekly declines (every one of the last 13 observations is lower than or equal to the prior, actually strictly declining), and stable policy language reaffirming continued runoff, the base case for month-end is a continuation of the same modest weekly decline, roughly in line with the 4-week average pace. Would change its mind if: A weekly print flat or rising, sustained reversal above ~8.0T, or policy language announcing a pause/taper/end of runoff.

Policy language shifted to acknowledge growth "slowed from its strong pace," a dovish tilt versus prior language, which typically supports front-end anchoring and continued 2s10s steepening as the market prices a plateauing tightening cycle. With five straight periods of higher or flat prints and momentum still positive, I lean toward continued steepening, though the pace of change has been modest and the spread remains solidly inverted, limiting conviction that this move accelerates sharply.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a steepener (long DGS10 / short DGS2) on a thesis of continued QT (balance sheet down, pushing long-end term premium up relative to front) plus resilient labor tightening keeping the Fed anchored at the front. That thesis has weakened materially this meeting. The curve_slope analyst itself has flipped from up (0.50) to down (0.35): the 2s10s spread rolled over from -0.14 back to -0.28/-0.30, 63d change is now negative, and short-term momentum has clearly turned toward flattening even though the multi-quarter trend and range position still show room. Term_premium (the long end proxy, DGS10) has broken decisively below 4.00% with accelerating downside momentum over the last three prints — a bull move concentrated in duration, not obviously curve-differentiated, but the pace of the decline (63d -0.89pp) is steep enough that it likely compresses versus the front end given labor_tightness is flat/noisy (unemployment whipsawing 3.7-3.9%, no clean trend, low conviction 0.20) and offers no front-end anchoring case for further front-end backup. Balance_sheet is still declining (down 0.55) but decelerating sharply (4w pace less than half the 13w pace), weakening the "QT drives term premium higher" impulse that supported the steepener. With curve_slope's own read now flattening and term_premium sliding hard while labor gives no offsetting front-end tightening signal, the balance of evidence has shifted from steepening to flattening since last meeting. This is a genuine turn in the primary slope driver itself, not just noise — I'm cutting the steepener size and flipping toward a modest flattener, but keeping conviction low given the still-elevated range position and short data window.

discounted.why: Noisy, low-conviction (0.20), no clean signal for front-end differentiation this month.

arbitrated.falsifier: A move back above -0.16 on 2s10s with 21d and 63d slope_change both turning positive, or a rebound in DGS10 above 4.10% with positive momentum, would flip me back to steepener.

risks.text: Long-term 252d steepening trend and high range position could reassert once near-term momentum stabilizes.
A QT pause/reversal would remove the flattening driver and could re-steepen sharply.
Reversing a carried position on one month of data risks whipsaw if next print reverts to steepening.
Loosening financial conditions (not my seat) could still pull long-end yields down further, amplifying flattening beyond slope fundamentals.

trade.rationale: Flip from steepener to modest flattener: short DGS10/long DGS2 equal-weighted, reflecting curve_slope's own reversal and term_premium's sharp long-end decline outpacing any front-end move.

arbitrated.notes:
Last meeting I ran a modest flattener (short DGS10, long DGS2) built on three legs pulling flatter: term premium falling, balance sheet still shrinking, and curve_slope itself calling flattening. This meeting's panel undercuts that stack materially. Curve_slope's own analyst now reports a stall: 21d negative but 63d flat and 252d still strongly positive (+0.50pp), with the last two prints actually giving back some inversion (-0.41 to -0.37/-0.39). They downgrade to "flat, 0.25" from "down, 0.35" — explicitly weaker and directionless-to-slightly-flatter, not a continuation call. Term_premium has flipped outright, from down/-0.45 to up/+0.35, on positive 21d momentum, mid-range level, and a modestly positive 30s10s slope — that's a long-end-firming signal that should mechanically resist flattening, not support it. Balance_sheet remains a firm down/-0.60, and continued QT is a genuine long-end supply headwind that argues for steepening pressure via more term premium, which is directionally consistent with the term_premium analyst's own up-call — both point the same way on the slope axis (steeper), even though they're reported on the level axis with balance_sheet negative (falling assets → higher long yields → steeper) and term_premium positive (rising 10y → steeper). Labor_tightness continuing to rise (loosening market) is front-end supportive of cuts eventually, a slow-moving steepener input, not decisive at this horizon. Financial conditions easing (down/-0.60) is a background risk-on signal, ambiguous for slope directly but consistent with no urgent flight-to-quality flattening the curve.

Net: the flattening case I held last time has clearly eroded — the analyst most central to my seat softened his own call, and the long-end driver reversed sign entirely. This is exactly the kind of "driver turned" evidence needed to resize, not just noise. I'm cutting the flattener toward flat/near-neutral, tilting slightly toward a steepener bias but not reversing hard given curve_slope itself is only "flat" with weak conviction.

discounted.why: Directionally supportive but too gradual/uncertain in timing to size the slope trade on its own.

arbitrated.falsifier: A clean break of 2s10s below -0.45 with 21d and 63d both re-flattening would restore the flattener; a break above -0.30 with continued term-premium strength confirms steepener.

risks.text: Curve_slope's own signal is weak (0.25 conviction, flat call) — trade rests more on cross-driver inference than direct slope evidence.
If Fed pivots dovish faster than priced, front end could rally sharply, steepening more than this modest position captures.
Reversing a carried flattener into near-neutral on a single meeting's shift risks whipsaw if term premium's up-call proves as fragile as its prior down-call.
Easing financial conditions could mask latent risk-off flows that would flatten the curve unexpectedly via flight-to-quality.

trade.rationale: Cut prior flattener toward near-flat given term_premium's reversal to up and curve_slope's own softened call; small residual steepener reflects balance-sheet supply and labor-loosening bias.

why.balance_sheet: QT continuing steadily, unchanged from last meeting; structural long-end supply pressure, mild steepening input via slope.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment trend up to 3.90%, 12m high; slow-moving front-end easing-cut signal, mild steepener bias longer-run.

Last meeting I ran a modest steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2, conviction 0.25) built on a strong balance-sheet-down (QT continuing to push term premium/long-end yields up) plus mild term-premium-up, against a weak curve_slope-down read and labor firming that argues front-end anchored higher-for-longer.

This meeting largely confirms the prior mix rather than overturning it. Balance sheet is unambiguously still declining at a steady pace (-0.60 -> -0.75 conviction), reinforcing continued QT supply pressure that should disproportionately affect the long end via term premium — a steepening force. Term premium itself has gone from a mild up-lean to genuinely flat/mixed: 21d momentum negative, level rangebound mid-pack, no confirmed break either way. This is the one input that's softened, and it directly touches the long end, so I discount my steepening conviction somewhat versus last time but don't reverse it, since balance sheet — the more informed, less stale read on long-end supply — still points the same direction.

Curve_slope's own reading is a modest re-flattening over the last three prints, but its own analyst flags this as within a still-positive 63d/252d steepening trend and low conviction (0.30). This is noisy short-term flattening layered on structural steepening — I weight the structural read higher given the corroboration from balance sheet.

Labor tightness ticking up (unemployment plateauing at cycle high, gradual loosening) argues for front-end anchoring/cuts eventually, which is steepening-supportive from the front end. Financial conditions stalling flat is a wash for slope.

Net: still a steepener, but with less conviction than before given term premium's softening and curve_slope's own short-term flattening tick. I hold position but trim conviction slightly.

arbitrated.notes:
Last meeting I ran a modest steepener (long DGS10 vs short DGS2, conviction 0.30) built on curve_slope +0.55, labor_tightness +0.55, term_premium +0.30, balance_sheet -0.50. This meeting's panel meaningfully changes the front-vs-long balance. Term_premium flipped hard: 10y yields fell from 4.28 to 4.09, breaking my own stated falsifier level (4.20) with negative 21d/63d momentum — this is a genuine long-end richening, not noise, and directly undercuts the steepening thesis's long-end leg. Curve_slope itself downgraded from "up" to "flat" (conviction now only 0.35, was implicitly stronger before): the last three prints (-0.16, -0.19, -0.20) show stalling/re-flattening after the steepening move, with slope_change_21d now only +0.09pp. Meanwhile labor_tightness continues to climb (unemployment 4.20%, accelerating), which is a front-end easing-expectations driver — that pulls toward steeper curve via lower front-end yields, but it's fighting directly against the long end richening at the same time, which is a flattening force. Balance sheet continues its slow, decelerating runoff — still a modest steepening/upward-pressure force on long yields, but its deceleration weakens that support.

Netting this out: the long end has richened more than the front end has eased, per term_premium's break of its own trigger and curve_slope's own downgrade to flat. The panel that previously supported a steepener has weakened on both counts — the analyst most responsible for the long end (term_premium) reversed direction, and the slope analyst itself downgraded conviction and direction. This is exactly the kind of "panel moved against the read" situation that should be respected. I am cutting my steepener toward flat/near-flat, keeping only a token steepening lean since labor_tightness still argues for eventual front-end cuts priced in.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a mild flattener (curve_slope +0.15 conviction, term_premium down, labor_tightness up, balance_sheet down) and held a small steepener-leaning position (long 10y, short 2y, tiny). This meeting's panel shifts the balance meaningfully toward flattening, not steepening. The curve_slope analyst itself now reports the steepening move has plateaued exactly at 0.00 for three straight prints, sitting at the top of its 12-month range — momentum has stalled, and the call is "flat" not "further steepening." Term_premium flipped from down to up: the 10y is reversing higher off 12-month lows with the last three prints rising steadily (3.84→3.87→3.91), even though medium-term trend is still down and 21d change is stale. This is a long-end-specific driver and directly informs slope: rising long-end yields off lows, absent a front-end catalyst, mechanically favor steepening pressure at the margin, but the magnitude is modest and the slope_change series shows the steepening impulse already exhausted at zero. Balance_sheet continues clean runoff (down, 0.62, unchanged conviction) which is a genuine long-end supply story reinforcing modest upward term premium — mildly steepening. Labor_tightness continues loosening (up, 0.35) but conviction is reduced since two flat prints at 4.20% suggest stabilization, not further acceleration — front-end easing expectations are not intensifying, capping steepening pressure from that side. Net: the strongest, most current signal (curve_slope) says the steepening has stalled right at flat; term_premium and balance_sheet still argue for modest continued upward pressure at the long end. I read this as slope roughly flat-to-marginally-steepening, materially weaker than my prior tilt. I'll trim my steepener toward near-flat rather than reverse it, since no driver has flipped to actively favor flattening.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel this month actually reinforces last meeting's steepener read rather than undercutting it, though with some nuance to add. curve_slope itself reports the 2s10s spread still up on 21d/63d/252d momentum (+0.15/+0.44/+0.64pp) and near the top of its 12-month range (0.86), despite a two-print pullback to 0.15 — this is a modest retracement inside an intact steepening trend, not a reversal, and importantly it hasn't broken my flip trigger of dropping below -0.10. So the direct slope evidence supports continued steepening.

Cross-checking against front-end and long-end drivers: labor_tightness is flat (unrate ticked down to 4.10 from 4.20, 3m change now 0.00), which is a modestly hawkish-neutral front-end signal — not the kind of sharp loosening that would force front-end cuts and force a steepener via the front end. But balance_sheet is clearly down (QT continuing, pace if anything slightly accelerating), which is a long-end supply pressure that should keep long yields firmer/higher relative to the front — supportive of steepening via the long end. term_premium itself is ambivalent (flat call, torn between near-term uptick and negative medium-term momentum), so I don't lean on it directly for slope, but the balance_sheet channel effectively substitutes as the long-end pressure argument that term_premium couldn't cleanly deliver. Financial conditions easing (not my seat, but background) also supports a Fed-cutting/front-end-anchored backdrop consistent with curve steepening.

Net: the evidence has not moved against last month's steepener call — if anything it's modestly reinforced by continued QT and intact 2s10s momentum. I'll hold the position, perhaps nudging conviction up slightly given the corroboration, but not sizing up meaningfully given the two-print pullback and term_premium's own uncertainty.

The 12m change of +0.30pp and sahm_gap of +0.20pp show mild cumulative loosening from the low, but the level remains only modestly above the ~4% natural rate estimate. Policy language shifted to describing the market as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," which argues against reading this print as the start of a fresh deterioration trend — it looks more like normal month-to-month noise within a stable band. Given the range-bound history, I lean toward the next reading reverting toward the 4.0-4.1% area rather than extending the rise, but the recent jump raises some risk of continued upward drift.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a steepener (short 2y / long 10y, i.e. long DGS10/short DGS2) built on: strong QT continuation (balance_sheet -0.68), a slope call of +0.35 (steepening), and rising term premium (+0.35). This meeting the panel has moved against two of those three legs. Term premium reversed hard — the analyst who called "up" now calls "down" with 0.45 conviction, explicitly noting the 10y grind lower over 21 days breached their own falsifier. That directly undercuts the long-end-led steepening thesis. The curve_slope analyst itself has also downgraded from "up" to "flat" (0.25 conviction), citing slope_change_21d turning negative (-0.09pp) and the print stalling in a 0.20-0.27 range — this is very close to my own stated flip trigger (slope back below 0.25, slope_change_21d negative) though not a full breach. Balance_sheet remains the one driver still firing hard in the steepening direction (down, 0.75 conviction, clean trend, no deceleration) — QT is a genuine structural front-end-favoring force (removes bid at long end, RRP/reserve drain, term premium upward pressure structurally) but the term_premium analyst notes this structural force is being "overwhelmed" by other flows pushing yields down at the long end. Labor tightness is flat/noisy, not curve-relevant here. Net: the long end is showing more downward yield pressure than the front end on a level basis, but since our mandate is slope not level, the real question is whether front end falls faster (steepening) or long end falls faster (flattening). With term premium and slope both flipping down/flat, and only balance-sheet still leaning steepening, I no longer have 3-driver conviction for a steepener. This is a genuine turn, not just one month's price noise — two independent analysts' own falsifiers triggered. I am cutting size on the steepener meaningfully rather than fully reversing, since balance-sheet QT continuation and 21d slope still nominally sit north of my hard flatten threshold.

discounted.why: Range-bound and noisy; no clear front-end differential signal for slope.

arbitrated.falsifier: A clean break of slope_2s10s below 0.20 with slope_change_21d staying negative, or balance-sheet runoff pausing, would flip me to an outright flattener.

risks.text: Overall level is falling (both term premium and inflation soft), which could compress slope regardless of QT.
Fed language change (steady rates, softer inflation framing) could shift front-end expectations unpredictably.
Reducing a carried steepener into a choppy, range-bound slope print risks whipsaw if the range holds and steepening resumes.
Term premium and slope drivers disagree on magnitude of reversal; true slope path may be noisier than either implies.

trade.rationale: Cut steepener size roughly in half versus prior (0.5/-0.5) reflecting term_premium and curve_slope both flipping away from steepening, while balance_sheet QT still argues against a full reversal to flattener.

why.curve_slope: Downgraded from steepening to near-flat; 21d change negative, choppy 0.20-0.27 range, longer-term steepening trend still nominally intact.
why.term_premium: 10y term premium proxy fell sharply, breaching prior falsifier; long-end yield pressure now downward, weakening steepener case.
why.labor_tightness: Range-bound, choppy, no clear trend; minimal slope relevance either way.

arbitrated.notes:
My prior call was a modest flattener (short DGS2/long DGS10 tilt, i.e., long the front end relative to back end) built on falling term premium and a shrinking balance sheet outweighing a flattening curve_slope signal. This meeting's evidence directly contradicts that framing on the slope itself: curve_slope now reports 0.57, near 12-month highs (range_position 0.91), with slope_change_21d/63d both +0.23pp and a 252d change of +0.83pp — a durable steepening trend, not the flattening I read last time. Critically, my own stated falsifier ("rebound above 0.38-0.41 with slope_change_21d re-accelerating positive") has been cleared and then some; the analyst notes it was cleared "long ago." I should not ignore that.

On the front end, balance_sheet is decelerating its shrinkage sharply (redemption cap cut from $25bn to $5bn/month, an 80% cut in Treasury runoff pace), which is a supply-relief/liquidity-easing signal for the front end — mildly steepening on its own, reinforcing curve_slope rather than fighting it. Term_premium continues down (10y yields falling, 4.32→4.17), which on the rate axis pulls the long end down — but this is a level move the Duration pod owns, and by itself is ambiguous for slope unless the front end falls even more. Given balance_sheet's front-end liquidity easing and slower Treasury runoff reduces upward pressure on front-end yields less than it reduces long-end supply pressure, but the direct slope reading is the most informed, cross-sectional measure here and should dominate a level-based synthesis.

I'm reversing my flattener stance to a modest steepener: the slope measure itself, my own falsifier condition, and the deceleration in balance-sheet runoff (front-end friendly) all point the same way. Term premium's decline is a duration story, not disqualifying for slope.

Given the clear recent momentum lower and the break of my own stated trigger level, I am shifting from flat to a modest down call, though I temper conviction since the slope didn't flatten as much as I'd want and the longer-window changes are flat-to-slightly-up.

So the prior "up" call was directionally correct, though the move was small. Looking at the full run: headline yoy has climbed from a 2.33% trough three prints back to 2.74%, a +0.42pp swing over three months, and the mom_gap_vs_outgoing is still positive (+0.04pp) versus the trailing 3m average of 0.19%. However, headline_3m_annualized (2.35%) is actually below the latest yoy print, suggesting the recent monthly pace, if it persists, would pull yoy back down toward the low end of the range rather than push further up — the yoy increase so far reflects base effects rolling off low prior-year comps as much as fresh momentum. Policy language shift acknowledging inflation risk has "risen" alongside employment risk is notable but doesn't specify direction of surprise. Net: mild upward momentum in headline persists but is not accelerating strongly, and the annualized pace argues for flattening near-term.

On balance, the dominant signal is a yield sitting at 12-month lows with a supportive policy shift, but the tiny recent uptick tempers conviction that the down-move continues cleanly into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 4.15 with 30s10s steepening further past 0.70 would signal renewed upward term-premium pressure; a fresh low below 3.95 confirms continuation lower.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Direct curve_slope evidence is now itself flat (0.58→0.55 plateau, 21d ~flat, 63d actually down 10bp), a material downgrade from last month's "up 0.30" steepening call — the steepening impulse partially fired then faded. That alone argues for trimming, not holding, the prior modest steepener. Cross-checking front vs. long end: labor_tightness strengthened (unemployment up to 4.50%, new 12m high, accelerating 3m pace) — a genuine loosening of the labor market that should pull front-end yields down on rate-cut expectations, a steepening force. term_premium's own read is "down" (10y drifted to 4.02, near 12m lows) but with 21d showing a stall/modest uptick and explicitly citing QT-conclusion as removing upward pressure on long-end supply premium — that's a factor capping how much the long end falls, i.e. mildly steepening on a relative basis if front end falls faster on labor-driven cut expectations. balance_sheet is still declining in the data despite the QT-conclusion language (effective date not yet reflected) — near-term this is a modest headwind to further term-premium compression, but the forward-looking halt announcement is itself expected to support the long end over front end once binding, a mild steepening tailwind on a lag.

Netting it out: the direct slope measure has stalled, but the underlying forces (labor loosening pulling front end down, QT halt language capping long-end downside) still lean mildly steepening, just with less conviction than last month given the failed breakout above 0.58. I trim size rather than reverse — the fundamental steepening case (cuts continuing, front end anchored lower, long end supported by QT-conclusion) is intact but weaker.
